request.question: What do both Norwegian Lundehund and Kerry Blue Terrier have in coman

title: Blue (Don Cherry's dog)
Norwegian Lundehund
Torums Scarf Michael
Chamuco
Storhamar Ishockey
Blue Paul Terrier
Kerry Blue Terrier
So Easy
Mariusgenser
Norwegian Confederation of Trade Unions
sentences: Blue was a white Bull Terrier owned by hockey commentator Don Cherry.
Blue was reportedly a gift from the members of the Boston Bruins when Cherry was their head coach from 1974 to 1979.
The original Blue, who died in 1989, was a female.
Cherry has since owned other white bull terriers, all of which were named Blue.
The Norwegian Lundehund ("Norsk Lundehund") is a small dog breed of the Spitz type that originates from Norway.
Its name is a compound noun composed of the elements "Lunde", meaning puffin (Norwegian "lunde" "puffin" or "lundefugl" "puffin bird"), and "hund", meaning dog.
The breed was originally developed for the hunting of puffins and their eggs.
Ch.

Torums Scarf Michael (aka Mick or Mike) (29 May 1996 – October 3, 2011) in Liverpool, England, is a Kerry Blue Terrier who is best known for being the 2000 Best in Show winner at Crufts, and 2003 Best in Show of the Westminster Kennel Club Dog Show.
He is the first dog to win the "Triple Crown" of dog shows, having also won the 2002 AKC/Eukanuba National Invitational Championship.
The Chamuco (a Mexican word which means "devil") or Mexican Pitbull is a dog breed not recognized by any Canofile association.

It originated in the center of Mexico, it was developed in this country in the 1970s, product of the accidental or intentional crosses of the American Pit Bull Terrier with the now extinct Mexican Bulldog, street dogs, American Bully, Pitbull Blue, Staffordshire bull terrier, American Staffordshire Terrier, and probably with the Boxer.
Its name comes from the Mexican slang "Chamuco", which means devil because of its temperament and tenacity.
Also it called Mexican pitbull or miniature pitbull, but actually is Dogo of Mexican origin.
Storhamar Hockey is a Norwegian ice hockey team based in Hamar, Norway.
The club currently plays in the GET-ligaen, the highest level of Norwegian hockey.
The club has won seven regular season titles and six national championships since its founding on 18 March 1957.
Storhamar play their home games in the CC Amfi.
The club also includes the largest junior department in Norwegian ice hockey.
The team colours are yellow and blue.
Blue Paul Terrier is an extinct breed of dog.
The Kerry Blue Terrier (also known as the Irish Blue Terrier) () is a breed of dog.

Originally bred to control "vermin" including rats, rabbits, badgers, foxes, otters and hares, over time the Kerry became a general working dog used for a variety of jobs including herding cattle and sheep, and as a guard dog.
Today the Kerry has spread around the world as a companion and working dog.

Despite a Kerry Blue winning Crufts (the most important UK dog show) in 2000, it remains an "unfashionable" breed, and is distinctly uncommon; however, it not as threatened as some of the other terrier breeds such as Skye Terrier, Sealyham Terrier, and Dandie Dinmont Terrier.
"So Easy" is a song by Norwegian duo Röyksopp, released as their first single.
It was first made available in 1999, with only 500 copies made and distributed.

It was later re-released on Röyksopp's debut album "Melody A.M." "So Easy" contains instrumental and vocal samples from a 1960s cover version of the Burt Bacharach/Hal David song "Blue on Blue" (often misheard as "Who are you") recorded by a Swedish vocal group called Gals & Pals.
The sampled lyrics are "Blue on blue, heartache on heartache/Blue on blue, Now that we are through."
Marius Sweaters (Norwegian: "Mariusgenser" ) are Norwegian-style knitted sweaters with patterns inspired by traditional Setesdal sweater (Norwegian: "Setesdalsgenser" ), but without lice.

While the traditional sweaters used the natural colors of sheep wool - black, gray, brown and white - Marius sweater designs incorporated colors, with the most common being red, white and blue, the colors of the Norwegian flag.

The Norwegian Confederation of Trade Unions (Norwegian: "Landsorganisasjonen i Norge" , LO) is a national trade union center, decidedly the largest and probably the most influential umbrella organization of labour unions in Norway.
The 21 national unions affiliated to the LO have almost 900,000 members of a Norwegian population of 5 million.

The majority of affiliated unions organizes traditional blue collar workers, but the largest affiliate is the Norwegian Union of Municipal and General Employees which makes up more than a third of all members.
LO is affiliated to the ITUC and the ETUC.
Gerd Kristiansen was elected leader on 6 May 2013 for a four-year period.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: What do both Norwegian Lundehund and Kerry Blue Terrier have in coman
hop1_docs: Kerry Blue Terrier | The Kerry Blue Terrier (also known as the Irish Blue Terrier) () is a breed of dog.  Originally bred to control "vermin" including rats, rabbits, badgers, foxes, otters and hares, over time the Kerry became a general working dog used for a variety of jobs including herding cattle and sheep, and as a guard dog.  Today the Kerry has spread around the world as a companion and working dog.  Despite a Kerry Blue winning Crufts (the most important UK dog show) in 2000, it remains an "unfashionable" breed, and is distinctly uncommon; however, it not as threatened as some of the other terrier breeds such as Skye Terrier, Sealyham Terrier, and Dandie Dinmont Terrier.

Norwegian Lundehund | The Norwegian Lundehund ("Norsk Lundehund") is a small dog breed of the Spitz type that originates from Norway.  Its name is a compound noun composed of the elements "Lunde", meaning puffin (Norwegian "lunde" "puffin" or "lundefugl" "puffin bird"), and "hund", meaning dog.  The breed was originally developed for the hunting of puffins and their eggs.

Fox Terrier | Fox Terriers are two different breeds of the terrier dog type: the Smooth Fox Terrier and the Wire Fox Terrier.  Both of these breeds originated in the 19th century from a handful of dogs who are descended from earlier varieties of British terriers, and are related to other modern white terrier breeds.  In addition, a number of breeds have diverged from these two main types of fox terrier and have been recognised separately, including the Jack Russell Terrier, Miniature Fox Terrier and Rat Terrier.  The Wire and Smooth Fox Terriers share similar characteristics, the main differences being in the coat and markings.  They have been successful in conformation shows, more prominently in America than their homeland.

Torums Scarf Michael | Ch.  Torums Scarf Michael (aka Mick or Mike) (29 May 1996 – October 3, 2011) in Liverpool, England, is a Kerry Blue Terrier who is best known for being the 2000 Best in Show winner at Crufts, and 2003 Best in Show of the Westminster Kennel Club Dog Show.  He is the first dog to win the "Triple Crown" of dog shows, having also won the 2002 AKC/Eukanuba National Invitational Championship.

What I Do | What I Do is the twelfth studio album by American country music artist Alan Jackson.  It was released on September 7, 2004, and produced four singles for Jackson on the Hot Country Songs charts: "Too Much of a Good Thing" and "Monday Morning Church" both reached #5, while "The Talkin' Song Repair Blues" and "USA Today" both reached #18.  Making it the first album of his career not to produce any #1 hits.
What Jamie Saw | What Jamie Saw is a 1995 novel by Carolyn Coman.
Blue Paul Terrier | Blue Paul Terrier is an extinct breed of dog.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Kerry Blue Terrier — [1]: "is a breed of dog."
- Norwegian Lundehund — [2]: "is a small dog breed of the Spitz type"

Ambiguities:
- None in the provided passages; each name maps to a single breed description.

Bridge / missing fact:
- None missing. The needed relation "what they have in common" (their classification) is present: both are dog breeds. Final conclusion: both are breeds of dog.

Retrieval direction:
- Page: "Kerry Blue Terrier"
- Page: "Norwegian Lundehund"
- Page: "Dog breed"
- (No deferral — passages unambiguously support the conclusion above.)
response.hop2_query: Norwegian Lundehund Kerry Blue Terrier breed
hop2_docs: Kerry Blue Terrier | The Kerry Blue Terrier (also known as the Irish Blue Terrier) () is a breed of dog.  Originally bred to control "vermin" including rats, rabbits, badgers, foxes, otters and hares, over time the Kerry became a general working dog used for a variety of jobs including herding cattle and sheep, and as a guard dog.  Today the Kerry has spread around the world as a companion and working dog.  Despite a Kerry Blue winning Crufts (the most important UK dog show) in 2000, it remains an "unfashionable" breed, and is distinctly uncommon; however, it not as threatened as some of the other terrier breeds such as Skye Terrier, Sealyham Terrier, and Dandie Dinmont Terrier.

Norwegian Lundehund | The Norwegian Lundehund ("Norsk Lundehund") is a small dog breed of the Spitz type that originates from Norway.  Its name is a compound noun composed of the elements "Lunde", meaning puffin (Norwegian "lunde" "puffin" or "lundefugl" "puffin bird"), and "hund", meaning dog.  The breed was originally developed for the hunting of puffins and their eggs.
Blue Paul Terrier | Blue Paul Terrier is an extinct breed of dog.

Irish Terrier | The Irish Terrier () is a dog breed from Ireland, one of many breeds of terrier.  The Irish Terrier is considered one of the oldest terrier breeds.  The Dublin dog show in 1873 was the first to provide a separate class for Irish Terriers.  By the 1880s, Irish Terriers were the fourth most popular breed in Ireland and Britain.

Australian Silky Terrier | The Australian Silky Terrier is a small breed of dog of the terrier dog type.  The breed was developed in Australia, although the ancestral types and breeds were from Great Britain.  It is closely related to the Australian Terrier and the Yorkshire Terrier.  The breed is called the "Silky Terrier" in North America, but is called the "Australian Silky Terrier" in its country of origin and in the rest of the world.

Scottish Terrier | The Scottish Terrier (also known as the Aberdeen Terrier), popularly called the Scottie, is a breed of dog.  Initially one of the highland breeds of terrier that were grouped under the name of "Skye Terrier", it is one of five breeds of terrier that originated in Scotland, the other four being the modern Skye, Cairn, Dandie Dinmont, and West Highland White Terriers.  They are an independent and rugged breed with a wiry outer coat and a soft dense undercoat.  The First Earl of Dumbarton nicknamed the breed "the diehard".  The modern breed is said to be able to trace its lineage back to a single female, named Splinter II.

Glen of Imaal Terrier | The Glen of Imaal Terrier () is a breed of dog of the terrier category and one of four Irish terrier breeds.  It is sometimes called the Irish Glen of Imaal Terrier or the Wicklow Terrier, and the name of the breed is often shortened by fanciers to just Glen.
response.summary_2: Both Norwegian Lundehund and Kerry Blue Terrier are breeds of dog.
